tate of Califor4ia
prt Political Practices Commissio4
P.O. BOX 807
September 15, 197 6
Roger Freet
Distr ict super intendent
Kings Canyon Unified School District
Post Off ice Box 552
Reedley, California 93654
Dear I"1r . Fr eet:
Senator Zenovich has provided our agency with a copy of
your letter of August 3, 1976, transnitting a resolution adopted
by the boarrj of your school district. 
Essentially, the resolution
asks the Legislature to amend the Political Reform Act of 1974
to exenpt elected school board members frorn the financial
disclosure provisions of Government Code Sections 87100, et
seq.
It is understandable that unpaid school board menbers
believe it is an imposition to be conPelled to disclose certain
of their private financial interests. Iloh,ever, under existing
Iaw, school districts are governmental agenciesi members of
boards of education as well as certain administrative personnel
within the school districts "make or participate in the making
of governmental decisions" within the meaning of the Political
neforn Acti and many of their decisions affect private financial
interests. Under these circumstances, the Act reguires that
school districts adopt Conflict of rnterest Codes applicable
to members and officials who are capable of affecting such
financial interests. only those financial interests $rhich
could be affected by the filerrs official actions must be
disclosed, and it is the school district which has the first
and primary responsibility to identify these potential conflicts
of interest an<i provide for their disclosure. Hopefully,
individuals who object to overly broad disclosure wilI take an
active role in the design of each coders disclosure requirements,
so that intrusion of personal privacy wiII be kept at a minimun
consistent with the objective of preventing or disclosing
conflicts of interest.
The Fair Political Practices Cornmission is required to
and has provided technical assistance to state and loca1 agencies
in drafting codes. rn particular, we have urged agencies to
avoid the easy path of imposing disclosure reguirements with a
broad brush but rather to oescribe with as much precision as
possible the particutar types of financial interests which
iould be affected by official decisions and which therefore
must be disclosed.
TECHNICAI. ASSISTANCE/AOMINISIRAIION (9I6) 322 5660
EXECUTTVE/|EGA| 322,5901 . ENfORCEi,IENT, CONfUCT OF TNTEREST 3226441


:i ' i.
\-/


\./
Roger Fr eet
September 15, 1976
Pag e Tlto
The Political Reform Act vras, of course, passed as an
initiative. 
Section 81012(a) of the Act provides that the Act
may be amended to further its purposes. Among those purposes
are the following:
Assets and income of public officials which
may be materially affected by their official
actions should be disclosed and in aPpropriate
circumstances the officials should be dis-
qualified from acting in order that conflicts
of interest may be avoided.
Gov. Code S 81002(g).
Given the circumstances, it is doubtful that the exemption of
school board members from the reguirements of the Act could be
construed to be in furtherance of the purposes of the Act.
Even assurning, however, that your proposal could be adopted
by the Legislature, and fu11y recognizing the right of school
board members to express their views, I believe the proposal
is rrithout merit. School boards make decisions of great con-
sequence to their communities. Like other public officials,
school board members serve in a position of public trust, and
the people have the right to be assured that their decisions
will be made free from bias caused by their own financial
interests. Disclosure of any financial interests which could
foreseeably be affected by their official actions is a reason-
able and eifective means of preventing conflicts of interest.
In recent years the California Suprene Court and the courts of
many other states have ruled that such disclosure requirenents
are valid and that any consequent intrusion of the privacy of
the official is justified by the important public interest
that is served.
I hope that Lhis infornation is helpful. If you have
further questions, please call Mr. Delbert Spurlock, Chief of
our Conflicts of Interest Division, at ( 9161 322-6444.
Sincerely,
rrlur 3Y*\
Michael Bennett
Executive Di r ecto i

Dear i'lr. Z lokovich:
senator Zenovich has provided our agency with a copy of
the resolution which was adopteo by the board of your school
district on August 5, 1976. Essentially, the resolution asks
the Legislature to amend the Political Reform Act of 1974 to
exempt elected school board members from the financial dis-
closure provisions of Governnent Code Sections 87100, et seq.
It is understandable that unpaid school board members
believe it is an imposition to be compelled to disclose certain
of their private financial interests. However, under existing
Iaw, school districts are governmental agenciesi members of
boards of education as v/e11 as certain adniinistrative personnel
within the school districts "make or particiPate in the making
of governmental decisionsn within the rneaning of the Politica1
Reform Actt and many of their decisions affect pr ivate financial
interests. Under these circumstances, the Act requires that
school districts adopt Conflict of Interest Codes applicable
to members and officials who are capable of affecting such
financial interests. Only those financial interests which
could be affected by the filer's official actions must be
disclosed, and it is the school district which has the first
and primary responsibility to identify these potential conflicts
of interest and provide for their disclosure. Hopefully,
individuals who object to overly broad disclosure will take an
active rofe in the design of each code's disclosure requirements,
so that intrusion of personal privacy will be kept at a mininum
consistent with the objective of preventing or disclosing
conflicts of interest.
The Fair Political Practices Commission is required to
and has provided technical assistance to state and locaI agencies
in drafting codes. In particular, we have urged agencies to
avoid the easy path of imposing disclosure requirements with a
broad brush but rather to describe with as much precision as
possible the particular types of financial interests l'rhich
could be affected by official decisions and which therefore
must be disclosed.

Even assuming, however, that your Proposal could be adopted

sections.question_synthetic: Should the California Legislature amend the Political Reform Act of 1974 to exempt elected school board members from financial disclosure requirements under Government Code Sections 87100 et seq.?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that exempting school board members from financial disclosure requirements would not further the Act's purposes and is without merit. School board members, as public officials making consequential decisions affecting financial interests, should be subject to disclosure requirements to prevent conflicts of interest.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains three separate but nearly identical advice letters to different school district superintendents (Freet, Ward, Zlokovitch). No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections with headers; question and conclusion extracted from letter body. Letter dated September 15, 1975 in Ward letter appears to be typographical error (should be 1976 based on content and other letters). Text truncated in original document provided.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Should the California Legislature amend the Political Reform Act of 1974 to exempt elected school board members from financial disclosure requirements under Government Code Sections 87100 et seq.?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC concludes that exempting school board members from financial disclosure requirements would not further the Act's purposes and is without merit. School board members, as public officials making consequential decisions affecting financial interests, should be subject to disclosure requirements to prevent conflicts of interest.

embedding.summary: This document comprises three nearly identical FPPC advice letters (dated September 15, 1976) responding to school districts' resolutions requesting exemption of elected school board members from the Political Reform Act's financial disclosure provisions. The FPPC rejects the proposal, explaining that disclosure requirements are necessary and constitutional safeguards against conflicts of interest.
